bm25.answer: 0cbccdf9 2204.08939v1-2: This paper proposes a novel deep learning surrogate modeling solution for rapid hemodynamic predictions in 3D patient-specific computational fluid dynamics. It addresses challenges in parameterizing complex 3D geometries and the computational cost of massive forward simulations by developing a statistical generative model for shapes, an unsupervised shape correspondence solution, an automatic simulation routine for data generation, and an efficient supervised learning solution to map geometric inputs to hemodynamics predictions in latent spaces. Numerical studies on aortic flows are conducted to demonstrate the effectiveness and merit of the proposed techniques. 2.3.3. Evaluation of performance: Defines the Relative Mean Square Error (RMSE) as the metric for evaluating the performance of the ML-based surrogate model. It specifies how RMSE is calculated for scalar fields (pressure) and vector fields (wall shear stress, velocity), including both vector and magnitude errors, and how it's averaged across multiple samples in a test dataset. 2.3.1. Encoding-decoding formulation.: Explains the use of PCA (or POD) for encoding 3D shapes and solution fields into low-dimensional latent spaces to manage high dimensionality. It notes that 100% of PCA total variation is preserved for input geometries and over 95% for output fields, resulting in reconstruction errors less than 1%, with MLPs learning relationships in this latent space.

2.3.1. Encoding-decoding formulation.: Explains the use of PCA (or POD) for encoding 3D shapes and solution fields into low-dimensional latent spaces to manage high dimensionality. It notes that 100% of PCA total variation is preserved for input geometries and over 95% for output fields, resulting in reconstruction errors less than 1%, with MLPs learning relationships in this latent space. 1. Introduction: Discusses the critical role of hemodynamics in cardiovascular disease (CVD) diagnosis and prognosis, highlighting the limitations of medical imaging alone. It introduces image-based computational modeling (CFD/FSI) but points out its high computational cost and numerical challenges. The chapter then explores reduced-order modeling (ROM) as an alternative, detailing its types (LP, 1D, POD-Galerkin) and their limitations, before introducing data-driven machine learning (ML) approaches, particularly deep neural networks (DNNs), as promising surrogate models for cardiovascular systems. 2.3.3. Evaluation of performance: Defines the Relative Mean Square Error (RMSE) as the metric for evaluating the performance of the ML-based surrogate model. It specifies how RMSE is calculated for scalar fields (pressure) and vector fields (wall shear stress, velocity), including both vector and magnitude errors, and how it's averaged across multiple samples in a test dataset.

2. Methodology: Outlines the proposed ML-based surrogate modeling framework pipeline. This includes reconstructing 3D aorta geometries, parameterizing the geometric space using statistical shape modeling (SSM), synthesizing virtual aorta shapes, automating CFD simulation data generation, and encoding vascular shapes and hemodynamic fields into latent spaces for deep neural network (DNN) training to enable rapid hemodynamic predictions. 2.3.3. Evaluation of performance: Defines the Relative Mean Square Error (RMSE) as the metric for evaluating the performance of the ML-based surrogate model. It specifies how RMSE is calculated for scalar fields (pressure) and vector fields (wall shear stress, velocity), including both vector and magnitude errors, and how it's averaged across multiple samples in a test dataset. 2.3.1. Encoding-decoding formulation.: Explains the use of PCA (or POD) for encoding 3D shapes and solution fields into low-dimensional latent spaces to manage high dimensionality. It notes that 100% of PCA total variation is preserved for input geometries and over 95% for output fields, resulting in reconstruction errors less than 1%, with MLPs learning relationships in this latent space.
